title: Firms' Inflation Expectations Return to 2024 Levels

source.name: New York Fed Liberty Street Economics

summary: The New York Fed's Regional Business Surveys show that despite substantial cost pressures in 2025—including sharply rising insurance, utilities, and tariff-related costs—firms' median year-ahead inflation expectations fell to 3.0 percent, returning to 2024 levels. Service sector firms raised prices by an average of 5.0% in 2025 (up from 4.1% in 2024), while manufacturers raised prices by 6.0% (nearly double the 3.3% pace in 2024). Longer-term inflation expectations at three- and five-year horizons remain anchored at 3.0%.
key_points: Service sector firms increased prices by an average of 5.0% in 2025, up from 4.1% in 2024
Manufacturing sector firms raised prices by 6.0% in 2025, nearly double the 3.3% pace reported in 2024
Firms' median year-ahead inflation expectations fell to 3.0%, returning to 2024 levels, down from 4.0% for service firms and 3.5% for manufacturers for 2025
Longer-term inflation expectations at three- and five-year horizons remain anchored at 3.0%
Firms interpret tariff-induced cost increases as a temporary, one-time adjustment rather than sustained inflationary pressure
Firms expect price increases to moderate to just over 4% in 2026, down from 2025 levels but remaining above 2024
why_it_matters: The moderation in firms' inflation expectations despite elevated cost and price pressures suggests that inflation psychology remains well-anchored. Anchored expectations reduce the risk of a self-reinforcing wage-price spiral, which is a key input for Fed policy calculus. If businesses and consumers believed inflation would remain elevated, they might alter wage and pricing behavior in ways that make inflation more persistent.
macro_context: The decline in year-ahead inflation expectations to 3.0%—aligning with longer-term expectations—suggests inflation pressures may be easing. This supports the case for the Fed to maintain or ease policy without concern that current cost pressures are becoming embedded in expectations. The data is consistent with consumer inflation expectations, which also fell to around 3% in early 2026.
notes: Source is a blog post from NY Fed economists based on Regional Business Surveys covering the New York-Northern New Jersey region. Findings may not be nationally representative. Published 28 days before TODAY (2026-04-01), well within the 90-day freshness window. Views are those of the authors and do not necessarily reflect official NY Fed or Federal Reserve System positions.

reasoning: Source is from credible institution (NY Fed) with clear methodology described
Publication is recent (28 days old as of TODAY)
Data is specific with exact percentages cited
Regional scope limits generalizability but is clearly stated
Authors are identified with institutional affiliations
